1.content:
Lately, I’ve been going wild with Claude Code + multi-agent workflows, and using git worktree a lot for development and code review. But one annoying thing kept slowing me down: every time I want to switch to a specific worktree, I had to:

    cd into the project root,

    run git worktree list,

    copy the path manually,

    then cd into the correct one.

It was tedious as hell.

So I asked GPT for help — and it came up with a dead simple but super effective solution using fzf:

git worktree list | fzf | awk '{print $1}'

You can even wrap this into a function and make it really slick. Here’s what I ended up putting in my .zshrc, which allows me to preset a few root directories (where my active projects live), collect all worktree paths, deduplicate them, and jump into any with just one Enter.

lw() {
  local roots=(
    "$HOME/dev/project-alpha"
    "$HOME/dev/project-beta"
    "$HOME/work/backend-service"
    "$HOME/work/frontend-app"
  )

  local all_paths=()
  for p in "${roots[@]}"; do
    if [ -d "$p/.git" ]; then
      all_paths+=("$p")
      if git -C "$p" worktree list &>/dev/null; then
        while IFS= read -r wt; do
          all_paths+=("$wt")
        done < <(git -C "$p" worktree list --porcelain | grep '^worktree ' | awk '{print $2}')
      fi
    fi
  done

  local selected=$(printf '%s\n' "${all_paths[@]}" | sort -u | fzf)
  [ -n "$selected" ] && cd "$selected"
}

Now I just type lw, get a fuzzy search popup with all my active worktrees, pick one, hit enter, and I’m instantly in the right directory — no more copy-paste madness. It’s a small thing, but man, it makes context switching way smoother.

I'll help you link Northbeam to your Route 53 DNS. Let me fetch the documentation to see the specific requirements.


favicon
Fetched Link DNS
northbeam.io

Based on the Northbeam documentation, here's how to link it to your AWS Route 53:

Steps to Configure Northbeam DNS in Route 53:
1. Access Route 53
Log into your AWS Console
Navigate to Route 53
Select the hosted zone for your domain
2. Create an A Record
Click "Create Record"
Configure the record with these settings:
Record name: i (just the letter "i" as the subdomain)
Record type: A - Routes traffic to an IPv4 address
Value: Enter the IP address provided in your Northbeam dashboard (you'll need to copy this from your dashboard)
TTL: 300 seconds (or your preferred value)
Routing policy: Simple routing
3. Additional Configuration if Using DNSSEC
If your domain has DNSSEC enabled, you'll also need to create a CAA record:

Click "Create Record" again
Configure:
Record name: i
Record type: CAA
Value: 0 issue "letsencrypt.org"
TTL: 3600
4. Save and Verify
Click "Create records" to save
DNS propagation typically takes a few minutes but can take up to 48 hours
You can verify the record is working using tools like nslookup i.yourdomain.com or dnschecker.org
Important Notes:
The subdomain must be i unless that's already in use (contact Northbeam support if you need a different subdomain)
Make sure your Content Security Policy (CSP) allows traffic to either *.yourdomain.com or specifically i.yourdomain.com in the connect-src directive
The specific IP address you need to point to should be visible in your Northbeam dashboard - make sure to copy it exactly as shown

1. Scope Overview

PetLab Group Limited (“PetLab”) operates an e-commerce platform that accepts payment via Stripe and PayPal, both PCI DSS Level 1 validated payment service providers.

PetLab does not store, process, or transmit cardholder data (CHD) or sensitive authentication data (SAD) on any of its systems. All CHD is securely captured and processed within the environments of Stripe and PayPal.

In Scope (for PCI DSS):

· Payment Integration Points:

o Our custom checkout pages embeds Stripe Elements (iframe) for card entry.

o Payment API calls from PetLab backend to Stripe and PayPal (using tokens, not CHD).

o Webhook handlers processing payment status updates.

· Stored Data:

o Payment tokens (e.g., Stripe PaymentMethod IDs) and last 4 digits of card numbers, used solely for operational purposes (refunds, customer service).

· Supporting Systems & Processes:

o Internal servers and services that store tokens and last4 data.

o Security controls to protect this non-CHD data.

Out of Scope (for PCI DSS):

· All cardholder data (PAN, CVV, SAD) handling.

· PetLab’s internal networks for CHD processing (no CHD handled internally).

2. Cardholder Data Flow

· Customers enter card data only into Stripe’s hosted iframe (Stripe Elements) on the checkout page.

· CHD is transmitted directly from the customer browser to Stripe/PayPal over TLS.

· Stripe/PayPal return:

o Payment token

o Last 4 digits of the PAN

· PetLab’s backend:

o Uses the token to confirm payments via API.

o Stores the token and last4 in its internal database (encrypted at rest).

o The payment tokens are used to recurring orders

o The last 4 digits are used to validate on the UI which card was used by customers

See attached C4 Architecture Diagram demonstrating:

1. CHD flows directly to Stripe/PayPal.

2. PetLab systems never see CHD.

3. Token-based integration.

3. Evidence Provided

a. Third-Party Compliance

· Stripe Attestation of Compliance (AOC) – current version on file.

· PayPal Attestation of Compliance (AOC) – current version on file.

b. Internal Security Controls

· Access Controls: Role-based access to internal systems holding tokens/last4.

· Data Protection: Tokens and last4 encrypted at rest; access via authenticated API only.

· Change Management: Secure deployment pipeline; code reviews for payment-related code.

· Logging & Monitoring: Retention of operational logs (webhook processing, API calls) for [X months] to support incident response and monitoring.

c. Policies

· Information Security Policy – includes requirements for use of third-party PSPs and handling of payment tokens.

2.content:
We are currently going from a PCI audit, and need to have the following evidence to prove we are compliant. The scope of this is anything that relates to card-holder data environments (CDE). I have added a doc to explain this further here: Petlab Group Limited – PCI DSS Scope.docx

The gist of it is, anything related to do with Backend API, CAP/CST/Checkout Apps, and anything that touches the sub engine database. so any of the documentation below, anything not in scope we should not supply, if QA server

This is the full list of what we have been asked for:

    An up-to-date inventory of in-scope system components (AWS and GCP) - can be a print screen or a simple spreadsheet listing out what's in scope

    1.3.1 - Inbound traffic to CDE restricted: 

        AWS config rules (screenshot), 

        AWS WAF rules (screenshot), 

        our security groups in AWS to prove that we've got this in place. 

    1.3.2 Outbound traffic from the CDE restricted: to provide screenshots of what it would look like if someone tried to access one of our hosting machines from the command line. GCP out of scope for this question.

    2.2.1: Hardening standards for all components are maintained: to provide AWS Security Hub and Amazon Inspector configurations.

    6.3.3 - Installing security patches: PL to send historic logs and security patches.

    10.5.1 - Logging: Confirm log retention, automated SIEM alerts, and log integrity: cloud front configuration screenshots.

    10.6.1, 10.6.2 - Time synchronization and log integrity:  to send the resources that are in the CDE and where we provide NTP servers.

    11.5.2 - File Integrity Monitoring (FIM): To ask Eslam if we're using the AWS CloudFormation and if we're meeting the minimum requirements in the AWS responsibility matrix. If we use AWS as a certified PCI DSS requirement we need to use their configuration which is described in the AWS responsibility matrix. We need to review this and see what we have in place in AWS and what should be introduced so we can assess if this is possible to introduce from a business point of view. If this is not possible then we have other options.

# Changelog

## 1.0.97

- UI improvements: Fix text contrast for custom subagent colors and spinner rendering issues
